one_line_takeaway: T2L reframes LLM vulnerability localization into detection+localization sub-tasks and shows that fusing runtime crash/sanitizer/debugger evidence via an Agentic Trace Analyzer is the decisive ingredient (0% localization without it, up to 54.8% with it plus divergence tracing), on a new 50-case ARVO-derived, difficulty-calibrated benchmark.
key_finding: On the base T2L-Agent (without Detection Refinement or Divergence Tracing), GPT-5 leads localization at 41.7% (detection 44.3%), GPT-4.1 leads detection at 48.0% (localization 38.5%), Claude 4 Sonnet reaches 30.5% localization (45.9% detection), and open-source models (Qwen3 235B, Qwen3 Next 80B) trail below 10% localization. Adding Divergence Tracing gives the largest, most consistent gains across architectures: GPT-5 reaches 58.0% detection / 52.0% localization (+13.7/+10.3 points), and Qwen3 Next 80B jumps +13.8 detection / +48.9 localization. The single largest reported number in the paper is Qwen3 Next 80B's peak localization under Divergence Tracing at 54.8% (cited in the abstract as 'up to 58.0% detection and 54.8% line-level localization'). Detection Refinement alone lifts most models modestly, with DeepSeek V3.1 the standout at 53.9% detection / 53.4% localization. The starkest ablation result is removing the Agentic Trace Analyzer entirely: GPT-5 and Claude 4 Sonnet both collapse to exactly 0.0% detection and 0.0% localization across all five crash families, which the authors use to argue ATA is the load-bearing component enabling any localization at all. Family-wise, Buffer and Memory crash types are easiest (concrete runtime cues push GPT-5 to 53.8% and 55.9% localization respectively in the base setting), while System/Runtime crashes are uniformly hardest (detection 11-21% even for top configurations, due to sparse/unstable traces). Parameter-tuning experiments found more 'thinking' does not help: GPT-5 Medium reasoning budget outperforms High (50.9% vs 41.3% detection; 41.6% vs 36.1% localization), and temperature in the 0.2-0.6 range barely matters (e.g., GPT-4.1: 51.0% vs 50.8% detection). Against transferred baselines on T2L-ARVO, T2L-Agent (base, no refinement/divergence) consistently beats AgentFL and AutoFL: e.g., GPT-5 localization 41.7% (T2L) vs 29.8% (AgentFL) vs 27.0% (AutoFL); GPT-4.1 localization 38.5% vs 26.2% vs 9.0%; the exception is that AgentFL slightly beats T2L localization for some Qwen configurations.
core_contribution: The paper introduces T2L (Trace-to-Line), a reformulation of LLM-based OSS vulnerability localization as a two-tier task (coarse chunk-level detection followed by fine-grained line-level localization), together with three concrete artifacts: (1) T2L-Agent, a hierarchical planner-executor agent baseline built from scratch (no LangChain/DSPy/LlamaIndex) whose core novel component is the Agentic Trace Analyzer (ATA) -- a module that runs vulnerable targets in reproducible Docker containers instrumented with sanitizers, debuggers, and static analyzers, and fuses crash logs, stack traces, allocation metadata, and static-analysis output into a single 'unified evidence block' fed to the LLM; (2) two additional agent features layered on top of ATA -- Divergence Tracing (exploring multiple parallel reasoning branches from the same evidence block and aggregating ranked candidates instead of committing to one chain of thought) and Detection Refinement (an iterative two-stage coarse-to-fine loop that re-injects LLM-flagged code fragments as additional context for a second pass); and (3) T2L-ARVO, a new 50-case benchmark derived from the existing ARVO dataset (which the authors profiled across 4,993 reproducible vulnerabilities in 250+/288 C/C++ projects), built via a dual validation pipeline (manual expert review + LLM-assisted checks) to be difficulty-calibrated and evenly stratified into 5 crash families (10 cases each): Buffer Overflow, Uninitialized Access, Memory Lifecycle, Type Safety, and System Runtime -- mirroring the underlying ARVO population's family proportions (49.9%, 35.4%, 11.5%, 2.9%, 0.3% respectively).
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model in the traditional sense -- the paper targets defensive triage: given an already-observed crash (from a fuzzer such as LibFuzzer/OSS-Fuzz with AddressSanitizer output) in a real open-source C/C++ project, the agent must localize the exact vulnerable source line(s) so a developer can craft a targeted patch. The 'adversary' is implicit (a fuzzer-discovered memory-safety bug), and the paper explicitly separates 'finding the neighborhood' (chunk/file-level detection) from 'pinpointing the line' (exact match against a ground-truth patch diff), arguing that prior LLM vulnerability-detection work stops at coarse function/file granularity and ignores runtime evidence, leaving a gap for real remediation workflows.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section 7): (1) T2L-ARVO has only 50 manually verified cases -- broad coverage and balanced categories, but sample size is constrained by the cost of human verification, and ARVO's underlying dataset structure was designed for human developers rather than LLM-agent evaluation, lacking fine-grained metadata that could aid localization; (2) although T2L-Agent raises localization from 0% (no-ATA ablation) to 54.8% (best full configuration) through its three innovations, cost efficiency remains a concern -- the agent operates within a $1.0 per-case budget via task-aware planning and early stopping, but scaling to thousands of real-world vulnerabilities would require significant optimization; (3) higher model 'thinking' budgets do not improve localization (Medium beats High for GPT-5), indicating raw compute/reasoning scaling is insufficient and that better ways to harness model reasoning are needed. The paper itself does not discuss possible overlap/leakage between ARVO training-adjacent data and evaluated models' pretraining corpora, nor generalization beyond C/C++ ASan-instrumented crashes to other languages or non-memory-safety vulnerability classes.

datasets_used: ARVO (4,993 reproducible vulnerabilities across 250+/288 C/C++ OSS projects) used as the source corpus

T2L-ARVO: new 50-case benchmark derived from ARVO, 10 cases each across 5 crash families (Buffer Overflow, Uninitialized Access, Memory Lifecycle, Type Safety, System Runtime), built via dual manual-expert + LLM-assisted validation
quantitative_results: Base T2L-Agent: GPT-5 44.3% detection / 41.7% localization; GPT-4.1 48.0% / 38.5%; GPT-4o-mini 44.3% / 22.6%; Claude 4 Sonnet 45.9% / 30.5%; Gemini 2.5 Pro 17.4% / 10.5%; Qwen3 235B 25.9% / 9.2%; Qwen3 Next 80B 37.4% / 5.9%
With Detection Refinement: GPT-5 52.4% / 44.5%; DeepSeek V3.1 highest overall at 53.9% detection / 53.4% localization; Qwen3 235B localization rises roughly sevenfold vs base
Ablation removing Agentic Trace Analyzer (ATA): GPT-5 and Claude 4 Sonnet both drop to 0.0% detection and 0.0% localization across all crash families
Reasoning-budget effect (GPT-5): Medium 50.9% detection / 41.6% localization outperforms High 41.3% / 36.1%
Temperature effect: GPT-4.1 at 0.2 vs 0.6 -> 51.0%/43.6% vs 50.8%/43.5% detection/localization; Claude 4 Sonnet 46.5%/43.0% vs 47.3%/44.9% -- largely stable
Crash-family difficulty (base setting): Buffer and Memory easiest (GPT-5 53.8% and 55.9% localization); Runtime hardest (detection 11-21% across top configs)
ARVO corpus composition: Buffer Overflow 49.9% (n=2,490), Uninitialized Access 35.4% (n=1,768), Memory Lifecycle 11.5% (n=573), Type Safety 2.9% (n=147), System/Runtime 0.3% (n=15)
baselines_compared: AgentFL (transferred/adapted: test signals replaced with crash-log evidence) -- e.g. GPT-5 33.1% detection / 29.8% localization, GPT-4.1 27.3%/26.2%
AutoFL (transferred/adapted: two-stage class/method query + locked-navigation localization) -- e.g. GPT-5 33.9%/27.0%, Qwen3 Next 80B 11.5%/2.6%

T2L-Agent (base, no refinement/divergence) outperforms both on most models, e.g. GPT-5 44.3%/41.7% vs AgentFL 33.1%/29.8% vs AutoFL 33.9%/27.0%; exception noted for some Qwen configurations where AgentFL localization slightly exceeds T2L
